topic: European digital speech and platform moderation controversies

articles.headline_en: Meta allegedly censors Hungary ruling party posts
articles.summary_en: Hungary's Fidesz party and supporters accuse Meta of electoral interference ahead of April 12 elections, claiming the platform restricted content from Prime Minister Viktor Orbán and party officials. Meta denies the accusations, confirming that Orbán's posts remain active, while fact-checkers have found no evidence supporting the censorship claims. The dispute highlights the role of social media platforms in political elections and their responsibility as content distributors.
articles.significance: This case illustrates ongoing tensions between political actors and social media platforms over content moderation during elections, with accusations of bias lacking substantiation from independent fact-checkers.

editorial_overview: The available search results contain only one article meeting the specified criteria for European digital speech controversies from the past week. The Hungary case demonstrates the broader pattern of political actors challenging platform moderation decisions during election periods, though in this instance the allegations lack factual support. The incident underscores the recurring tension between platform policies applied uniformly and claims of selective enforcement by political figures. Meta's use of third-party fact-checkers and adherence to EU Digital Services Act standards reflects the regulatory framework shaping platform conduct in Europe. Further reporting from established European news outlets on related moderation controversies was not captured in the available search results.
